narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've poured your heart into this journey, and we appreciate the strength and determination it takes to pursue your dreams in a field that needs more voices like yours. Your application included a solid LSAT score of 38, which is above the typical student's score of around 37, along with a middle-income family background and plans to attend full-time, just like most applicants. However, your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 sits below the average of 3.21, and your school deciles—ranking in the 2nd decile for both Year 1 and Year 3 grades—are notably lower than the typical 5th or 6th deciles for others in the dataset. The admissions model, trained on past students' paths to the bar exam, predicted a 66.5% chance that you would not pass the bar on your first try, which unfortunately led to the rejection of your application as the school prioritizes those likely to succeed right away. The most influential factor pulling in a positive direction—toward passing the bar—was your strong LSAT score, which stood out as a clear strength compared to peers. On the flip side, your Year 3 school decile ranking had the strongest negative pull, significantly raising the model's concern about bar exam failure because it was much lower than average. Your Year 1 decile followed closely as another key concern, again being well below typical levels and contributing substantially to the risk assessment. Your undergraduate GPA and family income bracket played smaller but still notable roles in heightening the predicted risk, as they fell short of the stronger profiles seen in students who typically pass. While your full-time status nudged things slightly toward success, it wasn't enough to offset the top concerns. This decision reflects how your academic rankings stacked up against the patterns of thousands of past students, not a judgment on your potential. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your deciles and GPA, perhaps seeking targeted LSAT-level prep to build on your strength there, and reapplying with an updated profile—many resilient applicants like you succeed on the next try. We're rooting for you.
